references.notes: The verse attributes agency to 'dahr' (time/fate), presenting it as a cosmological force that causes trouble, which is a pre-Islamic concept.
The phrase 'لل وتر طلابا' refers to the pursuit of vengeance (al-watr), which was a quasi-ritualistic duty in pre-Islamic Arabian society tied to honor and the deceased.

The poet attributes her burden and sleeplessness to 'khawālid' (persistent entities) that 'do not return to a returning place,' which in pre-Islamic belief could refer to jinn or spirits causing affliction.
This verse contains a wish for rain upon the grave of the deceased, reflecting a pre-Islamic mortuary practice and belief concerning the well-being and blessing of the dead.
This verse contains the oath formula 'لعمري' (by my life), which is a common pre-Islamic oath.
The verse explicitly mentions swearing an oath (حلفت) and fulfilling it (أبرت), which was a solemn and sacred act in pre-Islamic Arabian society, often implicitly invoking divine witness or sanction.
These verses explicitly describe the grave and burial practices ('ضريحة', 'صفائح', 'رمس', 'جدث'), which are components of pre-Islamic mortuary beliefs.
These verses personify Time ('الزمان') as an active, destructive cosmological force ('ريب الزمان', 'ام الزمان'), a key pre-Islamic belief related to Dahr.
This verse describes women engaging in lamentation and wailing ('يندبن نوحا'), a significant pre-Islamic mourning rite.
This verse refers to 'سوانح' and 'بوارح,' which are terms for auspicious and inauspicious omens derived from bird flight, a common pre-Islamic divinatory practice.
The mention of 'نائحات' (wailing women) explicitly refers to a key component of pre-Islamic Arabian mourning rites.
The verse describes the poet observing a bird omen (ṭīra) and specifically mentions 'saniḥ' and 'bāriḥ,' which are technical terms for types of bird augury.
The verse refers to 'al-manūn' as an irresistible force of fate or death, a common cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
This verse states that the deceased have 'followed Thamūd,' meaning they have met the same fate of perishing, which is a conceptual statement about the nature of death and human destiny.
The verse refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as a powerful, enduring force, which is a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The verse explicitly states the obligation of vengeance ('ولا صلح حتى نستقيد الخرائدا'), a fundamental quasi-ritual duty in pre-Islamic Arabian society related to honor and the deceased.
The verse refers to an 'ārid (omen bird or ill omen) flying at the time of death, which is a clear reference to pre-Islamic augury or omens associated with death.
The phrase 'among the graves' (bayna al-luḥūd) explicitly refers to burial practices, a component of pre-Islamic mortuary belief.
The concept of being 'hostage to decay' (rahīn balā) and the inevitability of decay for all youth expresses a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of the nature of death.
The term 'ṣarf' (turning or vicissitude) in the context of calamities refers to the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of fate or time as an active force.
The explicit mention of 'ṣurūf al-dahr' (the turns of time/fate) directly refers to the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of dahr as a powerful, often destructive, force.
The phrase 'al-dahr forcibly overcame them' personifies dahr (time/fate) as a dominant cosmological agent, a central pre-Islamic belief.
The phrase 'li-Llāhi darru' is an ancient Arabic exclamation of praise, functioning as an oath or invocation anchored to Allāh, who was a recognized deity in the pre-Islamic Arabian pantheon.
This verse describes the deceased as residing in a 'desolate place of spirits,' reflecting a pre-Islamic belief about the state or abode of souls after death.
The personification of 'الدهر' (Time/Fate) as an agent capable of taking a friend is a direct reference to the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of الدهر as a powerful, often impersonal, force.
The verse refers to 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an active, harmful force, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of dahr.
This verse describes 'al-Dahr' as having 'turns and changes' (صرفه حول واطوار), reinforcing its role as a powerful, fluctuating cosmological force.
The mention of 'al-Dahr' having 'sweet and bitter' aspects (احلاء وامرار) further characterizes it as an impersonal, influential force in human life.
The poet describes 'al-Dahr' as an unblamable, self-acting entity (ليس له معاتب وحده يسدي ونيار), which aligns with the pre-Islamic concept of dahr as an uncontrollable cosmic power.
The verse explicitly describes the physical aspects of burial, mentioning the 'locus' (locus) and 'burial mound' (rams) containing the deceased, which refers to pre-Islamic mortuary practices.
The term 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) refers to an impersonal, irresistible force of destiny or death, which is a key pre-Islamic cosmological concept.
The term 'الدهر' (dahr) refers to the pre-Islamic concept of time or fate as an impersonal, often destructive, cosmological force, which is lamented here.
The mention of 'بلا ثأر' (without vengeance) refers to the unfulfilled obligation of blood revenge, a central aspect of pre-Islamic mortuary belief and tribal honor.
The phrase 'عثرة كانت من الدهر' attributes misfortune to 'al-Dahr', a pre-Islamic cosmological concept of time or fate as an impersonal, often destructive, force.
This verse explicitly describes the burial of the deceased, mentioning the tomb (الضريح) and the materials used to cover it.
The verse speaks of death as an appointed fate and measure (منيته, وقت ومقدار) for every soul, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological understanding of destiny.
This verse reiterates the idea of death as an inevitable fate and destiny (منيته, أقدار), aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs about predetermined outcomes.
The mention of 'حفرة' (grave) directly refers to a burial place, linking to pre-Islamic mortuary practices.
This verse explicitly refers to 'ruqya' (incantation) performed by a 'rāqī' (charmer or exorcist), which was a pre-Islamic ritualistic practice for healing or protection.

The term 'al-Dahr' (time/fate) is invoked as an active force that can make things difficult for people, reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of Dahr as a powerful, often indifferent or hostile, agent of destiny.

This verse describes the sun eclipsing and the moon being misaligned as a direct cosmic reaction to the hero's death, reflecting a pre-Islamic belief in celestial bodies as omens or agents influenced by significant earthly events.
The explicit mention of 'al-Jinn' (supernatural beings) rejoicing at the hero's death directly refers to pre-Islamic Arabian beliefs about these entities.
This verse refers to 'al-qadar' (fate) as a force that overwhelmed the deceased, which aligns with pre-Islamic cosmological understandings of destiny.
The phrase 'ملمات المقادير' (calamities of fate/destiny) refers to the pre-Islamic concept of fate as an irresistible cosmological force, which aligns with the category of cosmological concepts.
The verse explicitly mentions the 'hām' (bone-bird), a pre-Islamic belief that the spirit of the deceased, especially if unavenged, would emerge from the grave as a bird crying for water.
The verse contains an oath sworn by 'al-Bayt' (the Kaaba), a central pre-Islamic sanctuary, and describes its visitors performing pilgrimage rituals towards the Jamārāt.
The term 'الدهر' (time/fate) is invoked as an impersonal cosmological force responsible for calamities, a common pre-Islamic concept.
This verse describes the deceased's fulfillment of the vengeance obligation (وتر), which was a quasi-ritual duty in pre-Islamic society tied to honor and the memory of the dead.
The term "المنايا" (fates/deaths) refers to an irresistible cosmological force, a common concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief regarding destiny and the end of life.
This verse refers to 'Time' (الزمان) and its 'vicissitudes' (ريب الزمان) as a destructive, inescapable force, which aligns with the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of Dahr.

The term 'الدهر' (Dahr) is used here, personified as a force inflicting harm, which aligns with the pre-Islamic Arabian cosmological concept of Dahr as an impersonal, often destructive, force of fate or time.
The term 'al-Manāyā' (fates/deaths) is used here as a personified cosmological force that brings about death, a common pre-Islamic concept of destiny.
The verse explicitly mentions 'jinn' as supernatural beings, indicating a belief in their existence and their capacity to experience loss.
The phrase 'صروف الدهر' refers to the forces of time or fate, which were often personified as a powerful, indifferent cosmological agent in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The phrase 'يوم نحس' refers to an inauspicious day, reflecting a belief in days having inherent qualities that can bring misfortune, which is related to omens and divination.
The phrase 'والله' is an explicit oath formula, common in pre-Islamic Arabian speech, invoking a deity.
The mention of 'رمسي' (my grave) and its splitting open refers to the physical aspect of burial and the state of the deceased, fitting into mortuary beliefs.
The mention of 'الضريح' (the tomb) directly refers to the burial place, which is a key element of mortuary practices and beliefs.
The term 'رمس' (ramasa) explicitly refers to the act of burial, which is a core mortuary practice in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The verse personifies 'الزمان' (time) as an active, destructive force that causes loss, aligning with the pre-Islamic concept of 'dahr' as a cosmological agent of fate and destruction.
The term 'هام' (hām) refers to the pre-Islamic belief in the bone-bird or owl that emerges from the skull of the deceased, particularly the unavenged, signifying a specific mortuary belief.
The phrase "ريب الدهر" refers to the destructive and unpredictable nature of time or fate, a central cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian fatalism.
The phrase "براها الدهر" personifies "dahr" (time/fate) as an active agent that causes suffering, reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of dahr as a powerful, often destructive force.

The mention of "التبل" (blood-money or revenge) being sought reflects the pre-Islamic tribal obligation of vengeance (tha'r), which was a quasi-ritual duty to honor the deceased and restore tribal balance.
This phrase is a common formulaic expression in pre-Islamic elegies, functioning as a wish or prayer for the deceased, implying a concern for their fate or memory after death.
This verse personifies 'al-Dahr' (Time/Fate) as an irresistible force that brings death, reflecting a pre-Islamic cosmological concept where Dahr is an active agent.
The term "المنون" (al-manūn) refers to fate or death as an impersonal, irresistible cosmological force, a common concept in pre-Islamic Arabian thought.
The verse personifies 'الدهر' (Time/Fate) as an active, destructive force, which is a key concept in pre-Islamic Arabian cosmology.
This verse contains an oath by the father ('wa-abīka'), a common pre-Islamic oath formula invoking a sacred entity (the father's honor or spirit).
The phrase 'al-raʾs al-ḥalīq' (shaved head) refers to a known pre-Islamic mourning ritual, indicating a specific mortuary practice.
The term 'نادبة' refers to a lamenting woman, and lamentation was a significant ritualistic component of pre-Islamic mourning practices.
This verse describes a pre-Islamic mourning practice where women would expose themselves to the sun as a sign of extreme grief, indicating a specific mortuary ritual.
The term 'al-manāyā' (fates/deaths) is used here to describe an active, relentless force bringing suffering and death, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological understandings of fate.
The phrase 'دعوى الآليل' refers to a specific ritualistic cry or lamentation, typically by women in distress or mourning, fitting the category of mourning rites or lamentation.
The poet personifies 'الدهر' (time/fate) as an agent that causes grief and curses it ('فبؤسا له'), reflecting the pre-Islamic belief in dahr as a malevolent cosmological force.
The phrase 'wailing women' explicitly refers to the pre-Islamic practice of lamentation and mourning rites for the deceased.
This phrase is a common pre-Islamic oath formula, swearing by the life of one's father, which was considered a sacred entity.
The term 'منايا' (fates/deaths) refers to the pre-ordained and inescapable nature of death, a significant cosmological concept in pre-Islamic Arabian belief.
The term 'المنية' (death/fate) here refers to death as an inevitable and powerful force, aligning with pre-Islamic cosmological views on destiny.
The personification of 'المنية' (death/fate) as dragging its tails in battle reinforces its role as an active, inescapable cosmological force.
The term "الدهر" (Dahr) is used in a way that suggests its pre-Islamic conceptualization as a powerful, impersonal force of fate to which all humans are subject.
The verse explicitly describes the deceased in a "grave" (رمس, جدث, ضريح), referring to pre-Islamic burial practices and the physical state after death.
The phrase "تالله" is an explicit oath formula invoking "Allāh," a common practice in pre-Islamic Arabian oaths.
The term "الأجداث" (graves) explicitly refers to the place of the deceased, aligning with pre-Islamic mortuary beliefs and practices.

The personification of 'dahr' (time/fate) as an entity capable of possessing 'inherited wealth' aligns with pre-Islamic cosmological beliefs where dahr was often seen as a powerful, sometimes destructive, force.

references.entity_or_term: حلفت برب صهب معيلات إلى البيت المحرم
references.notes: This verse contains an oath sworn by the Lord of the Sacred House, a pre-Islamic sacred entity, and references camels associated with pilgrimage.
The term 'صداها' refers to the pre-Islamic belief of the ṣadā, a bird-like entity believed to emerge from the skull of the deceased.
The verse explicitly mentions 'al-nāʾiḥāt' (wailing women), which refers to a specific pre-Islamic mourning rite.
The term 'al-dahr' (Time/Fate) is used here as an impersonal cosmological force, a common pre-Islamic concept of destiny.
The verse attributes the annihilation of her kin to 'al-dahr' (time/fate), reflecting the pre-Islamic cosmological concept of dahr as an impersonal, destructive force.
The explicit oath 'wa-llāhi' (by Allah) is a common pre-Islamic oath formula invoking a deity.
The term "الدهر" (Dahr) refers to Time or Fate, a significant pre-Islamic cosmological force often seen as destructive or indifferent, fitting the category of cosmological concepts.
